title: Sega Bodega Builds Pop Music From the Inside Out
author: Allastair Voss
body:
Pop music made by producers who are also artists is a different thing than pop music made by producers who stay behind the glass. Sega Bodega understands this distinction viscerally  his work as a solo artist strips out the professional distance and replaces it with something that can feel almost uncomfortably exposed.

[spotify:artist/1ZvF4Sgnre3Rk2CpiNy077]

Henry Earnest Dodd, who records as Sega Bodega, is a London producer who built his reputation working with some of the most forward-thinking artists in contemporary pop: SOPHIE, FKA twigs, Shygirl, Caroline Polachek, Oklou. He's part of the NUXXE collective alongside Shygirl, Oklou and Coucou Chloe, which is perhaps the most coherent aesthetic micro-scene to have emerged from UK club culture in the last decade.

His 2022 album harold is a remarkable piece of work. It sounds like someone who has spent years making excellent records for other people and then finally sat down to make one entirely for themselves  and found that the thing they wanted to make was bruised, melodic, structurally unpredictable, and covered in distortion. It's a pop album that keeps refusing to be a pop album, which is exactly what makes it so good.

Tracks like "Cuckoo" and "Hungry Hands" operate in that space between intimacy and disintegration that the best post-PC Music production has always occupied. There's a directness to the songwriting that his producer work doesn't always show  he's vulnerable here in a way that doesn't feel performed.

What's impressive is that the production doesn't overwhelm the emotion. Everything is in service of the feeling, not the other way around. For a producer of his calibre that level of restraint on his own record takes real discipline.

## The Distance Between Producing and Making

The distinction between producing for others and making your own work runs deeper than it looks. When you produce for another artist, the emotional content is theirs. Your job is to create a context that serves their vision, that amplifies what they're trying to say without substituting your own voice for theirs. Dodd has done this at a very high level for a remarkable group of artists. The production on Shygirl's records carries his fingerprints  the distortion textures, the bass frequencies, the way space is used  but it serves her project rather than his.

Harold is what happens when those production instincts are pointed inward. The same technical vocabulary  overdriven textures, processed vocals, rhythmic structures that reference club music without being fully functional for the dancefloor  now serves something more confessional. The bruised quality of the album comes partly from the sound and partly from the sense that the maker is present in the work in a different way than a producer-for-hire can be.

"Cuckoo" makes this legible almost immediately: the melody is genuinely pretty, in the direct way that pop songs can be pretty when they're not trying to be cool, and then the production corrupts it in ways that feel like the contradiction between wanting to be beautiful and refusing to let yourself be beautiful. That is emotional content expressed through production decisions, not through lyrical declaration.

## The PC Music Lineage and Its Extensions

The critical shorthand for Sega Bodega's production tends to reference PC Music, the UK micro-label and aesthetic movement that pushed pop's surfaces to hyperreal extremes. The connection is real  the maximalism, the use of distortion as texture, the deliberate queering of pop conventions  but harold is also a departure. Where PC Music at its purest was interested in abstraction, in pop as concept rather than pop as feeling, harold is more interested in the feeling. The emotional rawness that drives the album is not what that tradition was primarily about.

The SOPHIE influence is more direct and more specific. SOPHIE's work demonstrated that extreme electronic processing could be not just formally interesting but emotionally devastating, that the transformation of the voice into something barely human could be the most human thing in the room. Harold takes that possibility seriously. The vocal processing on several tracks  "Hungry Hands," "Cuckoo"  creates voices that are clearly synthetic and clearly in pain, and the contradiction does not resolve into abstraction. It stays painful. That is a creative decision about what the album is trying to do.

## harold as Character

The record is named harold, lowercase, and the name functions as a character rather than an alter ego. It's not a persona designed to create distance between artist and material. Harold is the name Dodd gives to the feelings the album is processing  a way of making them specific and external enough to look at directly. This is a technique borrowed from fiction: naming the thing lets you examine it.

What harold the character is going through is something like grief for the self, or for a version of the self that didn't survive some transition. The album is not specific about the biographical circumstances and doesn't need to be. The emotional logic is the logic of someone who has changed without fully understanding what changed and is making a record to find out. That process is audible.

## Why harold Is Not Just a Producer's Record

The risk of a producer going solo is that the record becomes a showcase for technique at the expense of everything else. Harold avoids this because Dodd is genuinely willing to be bad at things. The songwriting is sometimes awkward. The vulnerability sometimes overshoots the landing. These imperfections are structural to the record's honesty  they signal that this is not a controlled demonstration of what the producer can do, but an actual attempt to make something that matters to him.

He's been relatively quiet since harold, but the work he's done for other artists in that time has only confirmed that his ear is one of the sharpest in the room.  The production on Shygirl's Nymph, released in 2023, carried many of harold's textural instincts into a more disciplined context. The influence runs in both directions.

When the next solo album comes, pay attention.
